title: Medigap Premium Increases: Understanding Why Your Costs Are Rising & How to Respond
rawContent:
What Are Medigap Premium Increases and Why Do They Matter?

Quick Answers to Common Questions

  • **What is a Medigap premium increase?** It's an annual rise in the monthly cost of your Medicare Supplement plan, usually 3% to 8%.

  • **Why do Medigap premiums increase?** Factors include rising Medicare costs, insurer claims experience, medical inflation, and your plan's pricing model (e.g., attained-age).

  • **Can I avoid Medigap premium increases?** You can manage them by shopping for a different carrier, switching to a lower-premium plan type, or timing your enrollment strategically.

  • **Do all Medigap plans increase at the same rate?** No, increases vary by insurer, state, plan type, and especially the pricing model (attained-age, issue-age, or community-rated).

A Medigap premium increase is an annual rise in the monthly cost of your Medicare Supplement plan, typically ranging from 3% to 8%. Roughly 14 million Americans rely on Medigap, also called Medicare Supplement insurance, to cover the gaps that Original Medicare leaves behind, including deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments. For most of these enrollees, that coverage comes with a predictable but frustrating reality: the premium rises every year.

Most Medigap policyholders are on fixed incomes. Even a modest annual increase of $15 or $20 per month adds up to hundreds of dollars over a few years. When those increases stack on top of rising Medicare Part B premiums and other healthcare costs, the financial pressure becomes very real.

This article breaks down exactly why Medigap premium increases happen, how your plan's pricing model determines how fast your costs grow, and what concrete steps you can take, starting today, to keep your coverage affordable.

Why Do Medigap Premiums Increase Every Year?

Three core forces drive annual Medigap rate hikes. Understanding all three helps you evaluate whether your next increase is reasonable or a signal to shop around.

  • Rising underlying Medicare costs. Medigap plans are designed to pay costs that Original Medicare assigns to beneficiaries. When those costs go up, insurer payouts increase. In 2026, the Part A deductible climbed to $1,736 (up from $1,676 in 2025), and the Part B deductible jumped to $283 from $257, a 10.1% increase in a single year.

  • Claims experience adjustments. Insurers periodically recalibrate rates based on actual policyholder utilization. If a carrier's enrolled population used more healthcare than projected, premiums increase at renewal to offset those costs.

  • Medical inflation. Broader healthcare cost inflation flows through to Medigap pricing. Hospital services, specialist visits, and procedures all cost more each year, which insurers factor into rate filings.

According to the American Association for Medicare Supplement Insurance (AAMSI), typical annual Medigap premium increases range from 3% to 8% but that range varies considerably by insurer, state, and plan type. As KFF's Tricia Neuman has noted, insurers pass rising Medicare cost-sharing increases on to policyholders with limited transparency, making it critical to review your own policy rather than assume your increase is typical.

The Three Medigap Pricing Models: Which One Affects You Most?

The single biggest factor determining how fast your premium grows is your plan's pricing model. There are three, and they produce very different long-term cost trajectories.

  • Attained-age rated Premiums rise as you get older. This is the most common model. Each birthday triggers an automatic upward adjustment layered on top of any general rate increase the insurer files. Over 10 to 20 years, costs can escalate significantly.

  • Issue-age rated Your premium is locked to the age at which you enrolled. Joining at 65 means your starting rate is lower than if you joined at 72, and age alone won't drive increases going forward.

  • Community-rated Every enrollee pays the same premium regardless of age. This is the most stable model over time, since premiums only rise due to inflation and claims experience, not your birthday.

Consider a simple scenario: two people enroll in the same plan letter at age 65, one under attained-age pricing and one under community rating. By age 75, the attained-age enrollee has absorbed ten rounds of age-based increases on top of general rate hikes. The community-rated enrollee only absorbed the general increases. The gap can easily reach $100 or more per month.

Understanding which model governs your policy is the essential first step to projecting long-term Medigap plan affordability and deciding whether switching makes financial sense.

Medigap Plan G Rate Hikes: A Closer Look at the Most Popular Plan

Plan G has become the dominant choice for new Medicare enrollees since Plan F closed to new beneficiaries. Monthly premiums typically range from approximately $100 to $300+ depending on age, location, and insurer, with annual increases running 3% to 8% per AAMSI data.

One 2026 change hits Plan G holders directly. Plan G covers nearly everything except the Part B deductible, and that deductible jumped to $283 in 2026, up from $257 in 2025. That 10.1% increase means the out-of-pocket cost you face before Plan G coverage activates grew meaningfully in a single year.

For beneficiaries looking to reduce monthly premiums, High-Deductible Plan G is worth examining. Its monthly premium is significantly lower than standard Plan G, though you absorb costs up to the deductible before coverage kicks in. That deductible rose to $2,950 in 2026 (up from $2,870 in 2025), per CMS, so the trade-off is real, but healthier beneficiaries may still come out ahead.

Perhaps the most striking data point for anyone managing Medigap Plan G rate hikes: the same Plan G policy can vary by more than 100% in price between insurers in the same zip code, according to Kaiser Family Foundation research. Since plan benefits are federally standardized, every dollar of that difference is pure premium savings waiting to be captured by comparing carriers.

Does Anyone Regulate Medigap Premium Increases?

Federal law standardizes what Medigap plans must cover. A Plan G from one insurer covers the same services as a Plan G from any other, that consistency is enforced through CMS and NAIC model regulations. But federal law does not cap premiums or limit how much an insurer can raise rates each year.

That oversight falls entirely to individual state insurance departments. Some states require insurers to file actuarial justification before approving a rate increase. Others have minimal review standards. As KFF's Juliette Cubanski has observed, this leaves many beneficiaries, especially lower-income seniors on fixed incomes, at the mercy of their state's regulatory environment.

Three states operate entirely outside the federal standardization model: Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Wisconsin. Each has its own Medigap structure with distinct consumer protections that may differ meaningfully from what residents in other states experience.

For plan comparison and state-specific guidance, comparing Medicare supplements in your area is a useful starting point alongside Medicare.gov's free plan finder tool.

How to Compare Medigap Plans and Find More Affordable Coverage

When a premium increase lands in your mailbox, you have options. The first step is getting current quotes, not from a single carrier, but from several. Free tools and counselors exist specifically for this purpose.

  • Medicare.gov's plan comparison tool Allows you to compare standardized plans side by side across insurers in your area.

  • State Health Insurance Assistance Programs (SHIP) Free, unbiased counseling available in every state. SHIP counselors help you review your options without any sales pressure.

  • Licensed Medicare agents Can pull quotes from multiple carriers simultaneously, saving you time and helping identify carriers with stronger Medigap carrier rate histories.

Because plan benefits are standardized, the premium is the primary differentiator when comparing the same plan letter across insurers. Jesse Slome of AAMSI puts it plainly: shopping around at renewal time can save hundreds or even thousands of dollars annually. Many policyholders simply don't know this is an option.

When evaluating total annual cost, look beyond the monthly premium. Factor in the $283 Part B deductible (2026), and if you're considering Plan K or Plan L, note their respective maximum out-of-pocket limits $8,000 and $4,000 per CMS 2026 data. Plan N is another alternative worth pricing: it carries lower monthly premiums than Plan G in exchange for small copays at doctor and emergency room visits, which may represent meaningful savings for beneficiaries on a tighter budget.

Strategies for Managing Rising Medigap Premiums

Rising costs don't require a passive response. Three strategies can meaningfully reduce what you pay.

  1. Switch to a lower-premium plan type — Potential savings: $30–$100/month. Moving from Plan G to Plan N or High-Deductible Plan G can reduce your monthly premium, though it means accepting more out-of-pocket exposure. Run the numbers based on your actual healthcare usage before deciding.

  2. Shop for a different carrier offering the same plan — Potential savings: $100–$1,000+/year. Since a Plan G from Carrier A covers exactly the same services as a Plan G from Carrier B, switching for a lower premium costs you nothing in coverage. This is often the highest-value move available to current policyholders.

  3. Time your enrollment strategically — Potential long-term savings: Significant. The Medigap open enrollment period the 6-month window starting when you turn 65 and enroll in Medicare Part B, provides guaranteed issue rights. Insurers cannot deny coverage or charge more based on health conditions during this window. Outside of it, most states allow medical underwriting, which can result in denial or higher rates.

The underwriting limitation is the most important constraint to understand. If you missed your open enrollment window, switching plans or carriers may require passing a health questionnaire. Some states offer additional protections, including "birthday rules" that allow limited switching without underwriting, worth checking with your state's insurance department or a SHIP counselor.

For beneficiaries exploring ways to save money on Medicare supplement plans, the combination of carrier shopping and plan-type comparison is typically where the largest savings live.

Key 2026 Medigap and Medicare Cost Changes to Know

Staying current on annual Medicare cost changes helps you evaluate whether your Medigap premium increase reflects real underlying cost growth or outpaces it. Here is a summary of the most relevant 2026 figures:

As Medicare's cost-sharing thresholds rise, the financial protection Medigap provides becomes more valuable in absolute terms. The key question is whether that value still justifies your specific premium. Reviewing your plan annually against current figures, rather than assuming last year's decision still holds, is the most reliable way to stay ahead of rising costs.

For a broader view of what changed across Medicare this year, the full breakdown of Medicare costs in 2026 covers premiums, deductibles, and other key updates in detail.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medigap Premium Increases

Why does my Medigap premium increase every year?

Your premium rises due to a combination of factors: Medicare's underlying cost-sharing increases (both the Part A and Part B deductibles rose in 2026), insurer claims experience adjustments, medical inflation, and, if you're on an attained-age rated plan, automatic age-based increases that trigger at each birthday. These forces compound annually, which is why the increases feel persistent.

How much do Medigap premiums typically increase per year?

The typical range is 3% to 8% annually per AAMSI data, but increases vary significantly by insurer, plan type, state, and rating method. Some carriers have posted higher increases in specific years based on their claims experience. Your actual increase may fall outside this range depending on those variables.

Can I switch Medigap plans to avoid a premium increase?

Yes, in many cases, with one important caveat. Switching to a different carrier offering the same plan letter is often possible and can generate real savings since benefits are standardized. However, switching to a different plan type outside of your open enrollment period or a guaranteed issue event typically requires medical underwriting, which not every applicant will pass.

What is attained-age vs. issue-age Medigap pricing?

Attained-age pricing increases your premium as you age, it's the most common model and produces the highest long-term costs. Issue-age pricing locks your rate to your enrollment age, so joining at 65 keeps costs lower over time. Community-rated plans charge the same premium regardless of age and are the most cost-stable option long-term.

Which Medigap plan tends to have the most stable premiums over time?

Community-rated plans offer the most premium stability because they don't increase based on age. Among plan types, High-Deductible Plan G carries a lower base monthly premium (though its 2026 deductible is $2,950), while Plan N offers lower premiums than Plan G in exchange for small copays. The most stable choice depends on your health, budget, and insurer's rating model.

Does Medicare regulate how much Medigap premiums can increase?

Federal law standardizes plan benefits but does not regulate premium levels or cap annual rate increases. That oversight belongs to individual state insurance departments, so consumer protections vary significantly. Some states require actuarial justification before approving increases; others have minimal requirements. Check your state insurance department's website or Medicare.gov for state-specific guidance.

When is the best time to enroll in Medigap to lock in lower premiums?

The Medigap Open Enrollment Period, the 6-month window beginning when you turn 65 and enroll in Medicare Part B, is the optimal time. Insurers cannot deny coverage or charge higher rates based on health conditions during this window. Enrolling at 65 under an issue-age or community-rated plan can keep long-term costs significantly lower compared to joining later under attained-age pricing.

Medigap premium increases are driven by real, measurable factors, rising Medicare cost thresholds, insurer claims adjustments, and age-based pricing models that compound over time. None of that means you're locked into paying whatever your current carrier charges. Since plan benefits are federally standardized, shopping for a lower-priced carrier offering the same plan letter costs you nothing in coverage. Reviewing your pricing model, comparing current quotes, and understanding your enrollment rights are the three most effective moves available to you right now.

If you're unsure where to start, speaking with a licensed Medicare agent or a free SHIP counselor in your state can help you evaluate your options without pressure. You can also explore a full breakdown of Medicare supplement plan options to see how different plan letters compare on both coverage and cost before making any changes.

seoDescription: Learn why Medigap premium increases happen every year and discover practical strategies to manage rising Medicare Supplement costs on a fixed income.
